framework.reasoning: This paper contains no trained neural network and no gradient-based optimisation of learned parameters -- it is a fixed-hyperparameter linear Kalman filter (R, Q set a priori) plus classical rolling-window time-series statistics (AR(1), variance, permutation entropy, mutual information) and cross-correlation lead-lag analysis. The real computational stack is numpy/scipy/pandas/statsmodels; no deep-learning framework is actually required. 'pytorch' is selected here only to satisfy this schema's required enum field (which assumes an ML paper) -- it is not imported or used anywhere in the generated code, and this is called out explicitly so nobody mistakes this for a neural-network reproduction. No GPU is needed: the entire pipeline operates on time series of length ~284 (observational) or a few hundred timesteps (simulations), trivially fast on CPU.

module_hierarchy.description: Builds the order-3 Taylor-expansion state-transition matrix F used by the TVP-Kalman filter
TVP-Kalman forward filter and RTS backward smoother estimating the time-varying elasticity beta(t) and its first three derivatives

public_methods.name: filter
public_methods.signature: filter(self, log_x: np.ndarray, log_y: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: (x_filt [N,4], P_filt [N,4,4], x_pred [N,4], P_pred [N,4,4]) forward-filtered and one-step-predicted state/covariance sequences

public_methods.name: smooth
public_methods.signature: smooth(self, x_filt: np.ndarray, P_filt: np.ndarray, x_pred: np.ndarray, P_pred: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: (x_smooth [N,4], P_smooth [N,4,4]) RTS-smoothed state/covariance sequences

public_methods.name: estimate_beta
public_methods.signature: estimate_beta(self, x: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray) -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'beta', 'beta_prime', 'beta_double_prime', 'beta_triple_prime' arrays, each shape [N], from log(x), log(y) input series (runs filter+smooth internally)

module_hierarchy.description: Classical rolling-window eigenvalue-based EWS: AR(1), variance, permutation entropy, mutual information

public_methods.name: rolling_ar1
public_methods.signature: rolling_ar1(self, x: np.ndarray, window: int = 24) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: array of lag-1 autocorrelation estimates per rolling window, length len(x)-window+1

public_methods.name: rolling_variance
public_methods.signature: rolling_variance(self, x: np.ndarray, window: int = 24) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: array of rolling sample variances

public_methods.name: rolling_permutation_entropy
public_methods.signature: rolling_permutation_entropy(self, x: np.ndarray, embedding_dim: int = 3, window: int = 36) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: array of Bandt-Pompe ordinal-pattern permutation entropy values per rolling window

public_methods.name: rolling_mutual_information
public_methods.signature: rolling_mutual_information(self, x: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray, window: int = 36, n_neighbors: int = 3) -> np.ndarray
public_methods.returns: array of kNN-based (Kraskov-style) mutual information estimates between x and y per rolling window

module_hierarchy.description: Cross-correlation lead-lag analysis between beta (and its derivatives) and classical EWS signals; simulation lead-time-to-tipping-point detection

public_methods.name: optimal_lag
public_methods.signature: optimal_lag(self, signal_a: np.ndarray, signal_b: np.ndarray, max_lag: int = 24) -> Tuple[int, float]
public_methods.returns: (lag_in_steps, correlation_at_optimal_lag); positive lag means signal_a leads signal_b

public_methods.name: pearson_orthogonality
public_methods.signature: pearson_orthogonality(self, beta: np.ndarray, other_ews: np.ndarray) -> Tuple[float, float]
public_methods.returns: (pearson_r, p_value) between two aligned signals

public_methods.name: first_significant_deviation
public_methods.signature: first_significant_deviation(self, signal: np.ndarray, baseline_mean: float, baseline_std: float, z_threshold: float = 2.0) -> Optional[int]
public_methods.returns: index of first timestep where |z-score| exceeds z_threshold relative to a baseline window, or None if never exceeded

public_methods.name: simulation_lead_time
public_methods.signature: simulation_lead_time(self, ews_signal: np.ndarray, tipping_index: int, baseline_window: Tuple[int,int], z_threshold: float = 2.0) -> Optional[int]
public_methods.returns: lead time in timesteps (tipping_index - first_significant_deviation_index), or None if the EWS never crosses threshold before the tipping point

module_hierarchy.description: Generators for the six simulated dynamical systems with known tipping points (fold bifurcation, beta step change, beta linear decay, logistic map, Stommel AMOC, critical slowing down)

public_methods.name: fold_bifurcation
public_methods.signature: fold_bifurcation(self, n_steps: int = 250, tipping_t: int = 200, noise_std: float = 0.05, dt: float = 1.0, seed: int = 0) -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'x' (response series), 'r' (forcing series), 'tipping_index'

public_methods.name: beta_step_change
public_methods.signature: beta_step_change(self, n_steps: int = 300, tipping_t: int = 200, beta_before: float = 0.8, beta_after: float = 0.2, noise_std: float = 0.1, seed: int = 0) -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'x' (driver), 'y' (response), 'beta_true', 'tipping_index'

public_methods.name: beta_linear_decay
public_methods.signature: beta_linear_decay(self, n_steps: int = 300, decay_start: int = 0, decay_end: int = 250, beta_start: float = 1.0, beta_end: float = 0.2, noise_std: float = 0.1, seed: int = 0) -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'x', 'y', 'beta_true', 'tipping_index' (=decay_end)

public_methods.name: logistic_map
public_methods.signature: logistic_map(self, n_steps: int = 400, tipping_t: int = 133, r_start: float = 2.5, r_end: float = 4.0, seed: int = 0) -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'x' (logistic map series), 'r' (control-parameter series), 'tipping_index'

public_methods.name: stommel_amoc
public_methods.signature: stommel_amoc(self, n_steps: int = 300, tipping_t: int = 200, freshwater_forcing_rate: float = 0.005, noise_std: float = 0.02, seed: int = 0) -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'T' (temperature-difference box state), 'S' (salinity-difference box state), 'psi' (overturning strength), 'tipping_index'

public_methods.name: critical_slowing_down
public_methods.signature: critical_slowing_down(self, n_steps: int = 300, tipping_t: int = 250, lambda_start: float = 1.0, lambda_end: float = 0.0, noise_std: float = 0.1, seed: int = 0) -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'x' (relaxation-process series), 'lambda' (decay-rate series), 'tipping_index'

module_hierarchy.description: Loads NASA AIRS monthly regional time series (from local CSV export of a Giovanni-portal query, or a bundled/synthetic fallback), with region bounding-box metadata

public_methods.name: load_region
public_methods.signature: load_region(self, region_name: str, data_dir: str = 'data/raw') -> Dict[str, np.ndarray]
public_methods.returns: dict with 'dates', 'T' (surface skin temperature), 'q' (surface water-vapor mixing ratio), for the named region ('arctic', 'tropics', 'monsoon')

public_methods.name: region_bounds
public_methods.signature: region_bounds(self, region_name: str) -> Dict[str, float]
public_methods.returns: dict with lat_min, lat_max, lon_min, lon_max for the named region

module_hierarchy.description: Computes the Table-1/Table-2-style region-level summary statistics (mean |beta|, sigma_beta, Pearson correlations, transition counts, lead-lag tables) from a region's beta and classical-EWS time series

public_methods.name: compute_table1_row
public_methods.signature: compute_table1_row(self, beta: np.ndarray, ar1_T: np.ndarray, mi: np.ndarray, beta_double_prime: np.ndarray) -> Dict[str, float]
public_methods.returns: dict with N, abs_beta_mean, sigma_beta, r_beta_ar1, r_beta_ar1_pvalue, r_beta_mi, r_beta_mi_pvalue, n_transitions (beta''=0 sign changes)

public_methods.name: compute_table2_row
public_methods.signature: compute_table2_row(self, beta_derivatives: Dict[str, np.ndarray], classical_signals: Dict[str, np.ndarray], max_lag: int = 24) -> Dict[str, Dict[str, Dict[str, float]]]
public_methods.returns: nested dict mapping each beta-derivative name to each classical-signal name to {'lag', 'direction', 'correlation'}

public_methods.name: count_regime_transitions
public_methods.signature: count_regime_transitions(self, beta_double_prime: np.ndarray) -> int
public_methods.returns: number of beta''=0 zero-crossings

module_hierarchy.description: Runs all six simulated systems through both the beta and classical-AR(1) pipelines and reports lead-time-to-tipping-point, reproducing Table 3

public_methods.name: validate_one_system
public_methods.signature: validate_one_system(self, system_name: str, x: np.ndarray, y: Optional[np.ndarray], tipping_index: int) -> Dict
public_methods.returns: dict with simulation, tipping_t, beta_lead, ar1_lead, winner -- dispatches to linear-mode Kalman filtering for the two explicitly-linear systems (beta step change, beta linear decay) and loglog-mode for the rest

public_methods.name: validate_all_systems
public_methods.signature: validate_all_systems(self, sim_config: Optional[Dict] = None, seed: int = 0) -> List[Dict]
public_methods.returns: list of 6 dicts matching Table 3's columns

module_hierarchy.description: Reproduces Figure 1 (beta vs classical EWS overlay per region), Figure 2 (lead-lag bar charts), Figure 3 (simulation validation grid)

functions.name: plot_figure1_overlay
functions.signature: plot_figure1_overlay(region_results: Dict, save_path: Optional[str] = None) -> plt.Figure
functions.returns: matplotlib figure with one stacked subplot per region

functions.name: plot_figure2_leadlag_bars
functions.signature: plot_figure2_leadlag_bars(table2_results: Dict, save_path: Optional[str] = None) -> plt.Figure
functions.returns: matplotlib figure with per-region lead-lag bar charts

functions.name: plot_figure3_simulation_grid
functions.signature: plot_figure3_simulation_grid(simulation_raw: List[Dict], simulation_beta: List[np.ndarray], simulation_ar1: List[np.ndarray], save_path: Optional[str] = None) -> plt.Figure
functions.returns: matplotlib figure with a 6x3 grid (raw series / beta / classical EWS per system)

module_hierarchy.description: YAML config loading and global random-seed management

tensor_flows.name: Observational beta estimation and lead-lag pipeline (per region)
steps: TVPKalmanFilter.estimate_beta(log_T, log_q) -> beta, beta', beta'', beta''' [284] each
ClassicalEWS.rolling_ar1(T, window=24), rolling_variance(T, window=24) -> AR1_T[261], Var_T[261]
ClassicalEWS.rolling_permutation_entropy(T, m=3, window=36), rolling_mutual_information(T, q, window=36) -> PermEnt_T[249], MI[249]
LeadLagAnalyzer.optimal_lag(beta_derivative, classical_signal) for each pair -> Table 2 lag entries

tensor_flows.name: Simulated tipping-point validation pipeline (per system)

input.name: simulated (x_t, y_t) or x_t series with known tipping_index

output.name: lead time (timesteps) for beta vs AR(1)

steps: TVPKalmanFilter.estimate_beta(log(x), log(y)) -> beta[N] (or apply directly to x if system is univariate, e.g. critical_slowing_down)
Compare against paper's Table 3 reported lead times (39-153 timesteps) as a qualitative, not exact, validation target

model.transition_matrix_form: taylor_order3  # ASSUMED confidence 0.55: paper names this matrix but does not give numeric entries (SIR ambiguity #1)

training.note: Not applicable -- no trained/learned parameters in this paper. R and Q above are fixed filter-design hyperparameters, not fit to data.

evaluation.simulation_noise_std: 0.02-0.1 per system  # ASSUMED confidence 0.4: not given numerically in paper (SIR ambiguity #2)

runtime.reason: Core numerical arrays, Kalman filter linear algebra
Statistical tests (Pearson r + p-value), cross-correlation, optimisation utilities
Time-indexed series handling, rolling-window utilities, CSV I/O for AIRS data
k-nearest-neighbor infrastructure for the mutual-information estimator (mutual_info_regression)
Reproducing paper Figures 1-3 (beta vs classical EWS time series, lead-lag bar charts, simulation validation panels)

Directory to write results and figures

entrypoints.purpose: Convenience wrapper running both the observational AIRS analysis and the six-system simulation validation in sequence, reproducing Tables 1-3 and Figures 1-3

cli_args.help: Directory to write all result tables and figures

risk_assessment.description: The order-3 Taylor-expansion state-transition matrix F is named but not given numerically in the paper (Section 2.2); an incorrect F would bias beta's smoothness/derivative estimates and hence the lead-lag timing results, which are the paper's central claim.
risk_assessment.source: SIR ambiguity #1, mathematical_spec 'TVP-Kalman state transition equation', confidence 0.55-0.8
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement the standard factorial-scaled kinematic/Taylor chain matrix (position/velocity/acceleration/jerk analogue) as the default, expose it as a swappable function in transition_matrix.py, and unit-test the filter's behaviour on a synthetic series with a known analytic beta(t) trajectory to confirm the smoother recovers it to a specified tolerance regardless of the exact F convention chosen.

risk_assessment.description: The six simulated systems' noise magnitudes, exact forcing schedules (especially the Stommel AMOC freshwater-forcing trajectory), and simulation lengths are not given numerically, so this repo's simulations will not reproduce Table 3's exact lead-time numbers (39-153 timesteps) -- only the qualitative pattern (beta leads AR(1) when coupling degrades, but not for the univariate fold bifurcation) is expected to hold.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Document illustrative default parameters clearly as ASSUMED in configs/config.yaml, expose all simulation parameters (noise_std, forcing rates, tipping times) as function arguments so a user can tune them, and validate success by the qualitative pattern (sign and relative ordering of lead times across systems) rather than by asserting exact timestep matches to Table 3.

risk_assessment.description: NASA AIRS data access requires either a Giovanni-portal download (interactive/manual) or an Earthdata-authenticated API call; the exact spatial-averaging procedure used to reduce gridded data to one series per region is also unspecified.
risk_assessment.mitigation: data/README_data.md documents the manual Giovanni pulls needed (variable names, date range, region boxes) and data/download.sh is a documentation-only script (no auto-download, matching the LSEG precedent from a prior ArXivist run); a synthetic-fallback data generator produces plausible T,q series per region (calibrated to roughly match Table 1's reported |beta| and sigma_beta magnitudes) so the pipeline runs end-to-end without live NASA Earthdata credentials.

risk_assessment.description: Permutation entropy and mutual-information estimator implementations are named but not fully specified (exact algorithm/library, tie-breaking convention for ordinal patterns, kNN parameter k for MI).
risk_assessment.mitigation: Implement permutation entropy via the standard Bandt-Pompe ordinal-pattern method and mutual information via a Kraskov-Stogbauer-Grassberger-style kNN estimator (both textbook-standard, parameterised so k and tie-breaking are configurable), and unit-test each against known closed-form values (e.g. permutation entropy of an i.i.d. uniform series should approach log(m!)).

risk_assessment.description: Two of the six simulated systems ("beta step change", "beta linear decay") are explicitly defined in the paper via a linear relationship y=beta(t)*x+eps (Section 2.4), not a log-log power law. An initial implementation applied the log-log elasticity filter uniformly to all six systems, which caused a multi-hundred-timestep Kalman warm-up transient to be mistaken for a genuine regime-change detection on these two systems.
risk_assessment.source: Discovered empirically while testing SimulationValidator.validate_all_systems() -- beta_lead values were suspiciously equal to tipping_t itself, indicating detection at t=0
risk_assessment.mitigation: TVPKalmanFilter now supports two observation modes (mode='loglog' for the real T-q elasticity application, mode='linear' for a direct time-varying regression coefficient). SimulationValidator dispatches to mode='linear' specifically for the two explicitly-linear systems and mode='loglog' (after a positivity shift) for the remaining four, resolving the spurious-detection artifact and producing a Table-3 pattern that now qualitatively matches the paper (fold bifurcation won by AR1, logistic map tied, beta winning clearly on the two coupling-degradation systems).

risk_assessment.description: This is a statistics/filtering paper with no trained model, so the SIR's training_pipeline section and this plan's config_schema.training are both marked not-applicable; there is a risk that this gets misread as a missing/incomplete reproduction rather than an accurate reflection of the paper's methodology.
risk_assessment.source: SIR training_pipeline confidence 0.9 (high confidence that it is genuinely N/A)
risk_assessment.mitigation: README.md states explicitly and prominently that there is no trained model here, only a fixed-hyperparameter Kalman filter plus classical time-series statistics, to avoid any confusion with the quantum-computing paper's very different (trained-circuit) reproduction pattern.
